segments.text: And so he was easily aggravated.
And so a student says, Doc, Dr. Lindsay, should we drive our cars or some detail?
Doc said, that's a detail.
Then another one shows up his hand, and he says, ask him a similar type question.
I told you that's a detail.
And that went on, and then he just went.
After a while, he just turned around and took off.
And I said, what happened?
The students asked me, what happened to him?
But he was, I remember that real clearly.
Did any student ever try to play a joke or a prank on you as a teacher?
I don't know that they did.
I think one time we may have left somebody in the classroom asleep, you know,
after class or something like that.
Did any student ever try to bribe you for a grade or anything like that?
No.
No.
A lot of them coming around trying to get grades raised.
That's a common occurrence.
How would they?
I'll tell you one of the things that you might enjoy was when we first let girls in.
Old Dick Davidson over there was counseling one of them, and I happened to walk by the door.
And the old girl was just sitting there just boo-hooing.
And Dr. Davidson looked over and said, here comes Dr. Holland.
He'll help you.
He was ready to unload that girl.
Did you ever have a parent or any other outsider try to twist your arm on anything?
Yeah, I had one student, but he got over it.
He thought his little brother was sharper than he was, and we hadn't placed him high enough or something,
giving him tough enough courses, and it turned out he couldn't even handle what he had.
The only problem I had, I had the only problem with two or three profs.
The young ones that we hired, we hired one guy from, he had gone to Switzerland.
What's the capital of that place?
Bern.
Bern, Lucerne, Geneva, Lausanne.
I've forgotten which one it was right now.
I don't think we've hit it.
Anyway, it's a well-known university.
And every one of them that we got out of there,
they handled their situation.
The students, just so rough, that the students couldn't get along with them.
And the students couldn't get along with this fellow.
And so we had to let him go.
And since then, he got an appointment over there,
and he makes a point to always send me a card.
We had another boy, a young man that we got,
came to us from MIT, just as sharp as he could be.
And you could write a wonderful letter.
But his, and when you talk to him, you'd think,
this guy is just going to make an outstanding faculty member.
I hired him.
Well, Toby said, you know, that's the first guy that we've had
that got into a squabble with everybody in the entire department,
including all the mechanics downstairs, in less than one month.
And we had to finally let him go, and I got him a job.
I said, you people can handle somebody like this.
I can't handle him, because he's just too overbearing.
He needs a job where he put him over in a corner.
And another one I'll tell you is Bacris.
I had the opportunity, the first opportunity, to hire him.
He came in over there to get me to hire him.
And I had this young faculty that I had just hired.
Most of them were 10 or 15 years younger than I was.
And he came in there, and he had a list of books,
that were longer than most, many bodies' list of papers.
I said, how in the world do you do that?
He said, well, I'll just kind of sketch out a few things,
and I'll get somebody else to fill in those little details.
But he was, I could just tell that guy was so overbearing.
And I decided that I didn't care how good he was,
that I had to think of the fact that I had nine or ten other faculty there
that I wanted to develop.
And if I had somebody like that,
he would just try to intimidate them, and run all over them.
And so I declined hiring him.
I didn't care how good he was.
You've got to look at this thing.
Can you, are you big enough to hire somebody like that?
If you're real big, you can handle him.
But if you're no bigger than we were,
you just can't handle somebody that, I don't care how good he is.
Well, old Martell hired him.
And I saw Martell later, and he said to me, he says,
how in the world did you pass up that man?
I said, well, I decided I couldn't.
We couldn't work with him over there.
So I'm not sure what he says now.
But anyway, I missed my opportunity there.
Let's see.
One of the people I forgot to ask about,
and if we, this is a note to the typist
that moved this back up to where we were talking about other administrators.
Dick Winerdy.
Oh, he was really a good one.
Dick, I gave him a departmental position as professor.
Because he couldn't get anybody else to take him on.
He wanted to teach this little course in activation analysis.
So I made him a professor of chemical engineering.
And he was over in the dean's office all the time.
And so after he went with Calhoun,
I'd forgotten, he'd jumped me about something.
I wrote some letter to him that just really had a twist in it.
And I sent a copy of it to Fred.
And he called me.
He called me up and he said, boy, that just tickled the life out of me.
He said, but you know, I don't understand, Winerdy.
When he was working for me, I just didn't have any problem with him.
But he's giving me a lot of problems now.
And he said, I can't understand that.
And I said, well, what the problem is now, he used to be working for you.
He's working for John Calhoun now.
But the funny one was that when he got two different occasions,
when he was already out.
And he called me.
And you can tell what's coming.
He says, Charlie, being on the department staff of chemical engineering
was one of the highest honors that I've ever had.
I said, my boy wants to get into graduate school.
Could you help me?
And then another one was whenever he finally got Crosswise over there
and he was looking for a home, I thought we were going to get him back.
Because, see, he's on my faculty.
And that's when the...
My faculty. Mm-hmm.
And so he was calling up wanting to come back.
That's right, when Dr. Miller became president.
Yeah.
Because...
He'd been Crosswise with some of those.
And, see, Dick and I had offices that adjoined each other.
And the first three days that Dr. Miller was president,
he didn't even say good morning to Dick.
He literally did not say a word to him.
Dick came into my office and said to me, tersely,
he said, I've got to find some work somewhere.
Well, he called me.
And I told him, well, you know, we'll try to use you.
Come on over, because I knew we were obligated to.
Now, when he was working for Dean Benson, what was his task?
When he was still in engineering, what was his task?
Well, I think he had him doing...
Fred put more stock in what he told him than I would have.
He kind of had Fred buffaloed a little bit.
Fred would kind of fall for some of that.
Was he to try to bring in...
Was he to try to bring in research money?
Well, he was involved in a lot of the...
It was more in research than anything else.
But he got stuck on him and Newell both.
And I could tell Newell was no good the day that he came in
when he was looking for a job.
And I made a couple suggestions to him,
and he just brushed them off.
And I could tell that he wasn't going to make it.
But Fred did...
I went along with him quite a while,
and he was subject to being sucked in a little bit.
Now, Wayne Erdy had that activation analysis.
He didn't have it down where he could...
I've forgotten.
Paul Crawford used to laugh about it.
He was looking...
He had his system fixed so he could shove these samples
kind of like J.C. Penney's all over the place,
but he couldn't handle it, couldn't find silicon in carborundum,
which is made out of...
He got in the cart before the horse.
Back to Joey's pleasures of being the department head.
What are some other achievements that you remember
during those years that you're proud to look back on?
You said teaching was the biggest.
Well, I got a lot of books published.
I got seven books published,
and I got a lot of them in distillation, kinetics, catalysis.
In 93, I wrote one on quantitative cancer,
modeling, and risk assessment with Silken, who's a statistician.
And so I enjoy very much the development of ideas on paper.
And, see, when I became department head,
I realized that I didn't have time to be down in that lab working all the time.
I just couldn't do it.
And so what I did do was get into this other field,
and I got started with Exxon down there in 1955
because I had, from a master's...
a master's degree, had worked on, with Phil Murdoch,
on algebraic solution of very simple distillation problems.
And it was just three or four components.
And I got down there, and O.B.
Franklin said,
well, since you're an expert on distillation,
we're going to let you work out this big old tower, pipe still.
And it had about 30 or 40 plates and probably 500 components
and streams going off everywhere.
And actually, in the wintertime,
and in the wintertime, and in the wintertime, and in the wintertime,
the way we were looking at them, when you're doing finite differences,
every time you come to a terminal condition,
you've got another set of boundary conditions.
I would have...
And so they gave me that problem.
And I just walked around for about two weeks
trying to figure out how in the world...
I can't even solve that little one.
How in the world can I solve anything like this?
So I finally found out a way to do it,
and that's what got me started on all this other stuff.
And they eventually were the first ones to develop a program on it,
which we call the Theta Method.
And it is still a real good...
method, and it has some theoretical basis.
And then I wrote a book on kinetics,
and it's still being used over there.
The one on kinetics is used,
and also we're using the one on distillation.
So those two books are still in use.
What were your guidelines for being a good department head?
Oh, I don't know.
I just tried to treat everybody fair.
That was all.
It just kind of...
I'm going to treat them like you'd like to be treated yourself,
as near as you can.
And it's hard to do that,
but to not have any...
be favoritism over one or the other,
try to have an open door to any of them
that had any problem,
and have some concern.
It's very difficult,
but that's what I tried to do,
and tried to help them as much as I could
with what helped them advance their own careers.
And you obviously had some graduate students.
Which are the graduate students you're most proud to claim?
Oh, let's see.
There were several of them that had done real well.
Norman Tetlow was one of them who went to Dow
that developed a whole design package for them.
And Ron McDaniel was also with him down there.
And... And...
And... And...
And... And...
And... And...
And... And...
And... And...
And... And...
And... And...
And... And...
Then Narker, I don't know, he was a graduate student,
worked over at the Center some,
was another good one.
I've had a lot of good graduate students.
And so... And some of them very, very good.
In fact, I had two, Dan Taylor and Park Davis,
that both went to Rice.
And I got them as graduate students.
And they also worked with...
took some courses under Dr. Kleppel.
He said that they were...
two of the best mathematical minds that he had seen come through from engineering.
That they just, they could handle it, and they were good students.
With what other institutions did you most closely cooperate?
Oh, I did some with the University of Texas, but not anything in real detail.
But Ben Winkle over there was, he worked in distillation, but he did more of the experimental
homework, and I was, I'd gotten off into the other design, it's really design methods.
I'd gotten into that because that's all a department head's got time to do, and also
with Bill Akers down at Rice, you may have known of him, he was, he went to Tech and
later went on and got his degrees at Rice and stayed down there with the administration
for a good long while.
As the enrollment at A&M began to change.
Yeah.
Women were admitted, CORE became volunteer, and so forth, did that, how did that affect
the department?
I, the only thing that affected us was whenever the enrollment got away, and we just were
looking at the possibility of having to run labs night and day, and all that kind of thing.
That was the only thing, and that kind of came to an end, and we didn't have the space
for it.
But other than that, I'd say that over the years, that the staff, the students, they
have really gotten better.
I've got a real excellent class right now.
I remember when I first started teaching, there was a tendency for students to think,
well, by the time we get to be seniors, we've already got the fundamental knowledge.
All we're going to do from here on is think, and solve problems.
But they didn't have it all, and they didn't, um, if you gave them something new, they'd
They'd say, well, we haven't had that, you know, as if that weren't fair territory.
But the students now just take it.
Anything comes at them.
Do you think that the courses that you and your colleagues teach now are more demanding?
Oh, yeah, they are.
And they're higher quality.
Actually, I was disappointed when I got my degree in chemical engineering
because I'd had a pretty good background in mathematics and science,
and they weren't using it.
They were just using mainly arithmetic.
And they made it hard but just giving you catchy problems.
That's why they kept the grades down.
But the material they really covered wasn't really all that hard, the fundamentals.
Another thing we had was open book, which encouraged you not to do anything,
which is not near as good as thinking that you're going to have to go in there
and have it all in your mind so that you can spend your time working the problems
through the thumb and through the book.
And when you give open book quizzes,
what happens is that you give something they can't find in there anyway.
And so I think it's better.
After that, I never did give open book quizzes.
I just felt like that you can, students will do better if they have to.
There's certain tools that you're going to have to learn regardless.
And just well learn them.
It may be drudgery, but there's certain, a carpenter's got to have a hammer and a saw,
and if you're going to do any good, you need something.
You've got to have a hammer and a saw.
And if you're going to do any good, you need something.
And if you're going to do any good, you need something.
There's certain fundamentals that you understand thoroughly,
and you don't have to be looking them up.
As women began to be in the classroom,
did you all have any examples of where somebody forgot
that he was speaking now to a mixed audience and said something?
I don't know, but I got one of these reports back once,
and I could never figure out what I had done that was sexy or something.
And it baffled me.
I've never been called on it but once, but I don't know.
What it was, it must have been somebody gave a poor grade
because I've never been called on it before,
and I never tell anything in class that's off color.
The only thing that I did tell, I did show them a film that I had,
some slides that I had made, and that might have been,
that's the only thing I could figure.
I was teaching a course on quantitative cancer modeling and risk assessment, and going back,
and I knew it was on, and I was given a little bit on waste disposal.
The first garbage disposal place was right outside of Jerusalem.
And it's the Greek name now that's for hell.
It means the same thing.
It was a mess.
And I was, that was one of the things I pointed out.
But before I did that, I went back and pointed out that probably,
hazardous waste incineration, or incineration began about 500,000 years ago.
And what I had was, I got somebody to draw me this cartoon and it showed these women
were logging, were lugging the wood on, throwing it on the fire and the men were all sitting
back watching them.
And I said, boy, in those days, men had it made.
And that's the only thing that I can figure out that I might have said that I never did
know what it was.
But in general, I'll tell you, the girls, what'll happen is only the good ones will
stay in.
The poor ones that see they can't make it, they're not like the boys.
The poor ones will drop out and go take another major.
You got some boys that'll stay in there until the bitter end.
And that's the difference you see.
Usually the girls are near the top of the class.
As far as you and your colleagues knew, was chemical engineering one of the
most demanding majors on the campus, in terms of power?
Well, I think electrical was real demanding.
I think chemical and electrical.
And mechanical was fairly demanding.
Those three were pretty demanding.
I'd say electrical maybe.
Electrical and chemical were probably two of the most demanding.
And I don't know, I imagine it would depend on your aptitude as to which you would think
would be the most demanding.
That reminds me to go way back to the 80s.
I think it was the 80s.
I think it was the 80s.
I think it was the early 60s again, as you and I and others were part of that committee
recommending the new name for this institution.
Did you and your colleagues in engineering have a preference for what the name ought
to be or ought not to be?
You know, I haven't thought about it since then.
No, I don't really.
I don't know that at that time that we would have gone with Texas A&M University, though.
I understand.
I understand.
Yeah.
I understand that.
I understand that.
And then the president over there, then Vice President Hall, he said that Mr.
Rudder could
not at all accept losing the A&M.
And our committee, remember, recommended Texas State University.
Yeah.
Because everybody else was going that way.
Right.
And he said, Mr.
Rudder just didn't like that at all.
And Dr. Hall said, he's the one that came up with what it now is, the A&M with no periods
and so forth.
Mm-hm.
And as I look back, okay.
Everybody knows what.
school it is yeah and it's a no it doesn't mean that anymore no that's right and so i think it
was a good deal going that that's what i'm saying is it doesn't hurt to be different all right the
when did we begin to get large numbers of international students in engineering
oh we started getting them way back there in the 60s some and i would not i was trying to at one
time we had some pressure on us to not have more than 30 percent in engineering graduate students
and i kept mine down and uh i tried to keep them down we could have had a bigger class of graduate
students and one of the things that we did to try to attract others would take these that had
finished with a four-year degree in science from some of these teaching colleges and get them in
give them a a fast course but that's when it started
and now there's just probably no uh no holds barred you can have any number you want to
there was some concern that the legislature would be concerned about us educating so many
foreign students and that prevailed uh a long time through engineering over there and we did
keep ours down what would you be your evaluation of the quality of the international students
the academic ability oh i think they're good
uh but i another thing that i think is uh that they are going to be the leaders unless we
uh i think that we might want to give some favoritism to ours and because what's happening
to our own students now is that they can get a good salary when they get the bs degree so they're
going out and taking that job and i'm not so sure but what there's nothing wrong with being
showing favoritism over your own
national students so that all of us can be able to give Конflicts to our own students
to our own
national students or their own educational
law you're a president of the state of the state or the state of the nation

we should have enough staff in more to attract a lot of good american students because they're the ones a lot of those are going to end up as director of your research head of your chemical engineering departments throughout the country and so we ought to be doing more to attract those people and uh that it is we all have them all on the same part and generally that's correct if they're all citizens but if they're not going to be any further universities that's why they're not going to be called a business college
bachmann and he's going to be going to be a professor the zionist
but the other science teacher
the zionist and the division of study
too bachmann that's going to be going to be a professor
um if they're not citizens we don't have to do that and i think for our own good we need to
we need to get some that are some of our own in line all right i've now just anointed you
as the advisor to chemical engineering planning the future and then to engineering what advice
would you give to your department and to your college colleagues as they look to the future
well we did a big study and i had all industry helping me on this and one of the things that
we came up with was what they needed to start looking at was to make sure that these students are
have a better rounded education chemistry are only taking about 10 hours
above the masters i mean above the bachelor for the phd the classroom is one of the best places
to pick up and to learn another discipline and what we need is people
that are broadly trained and they can even have some business and this is not giving up the
english and this sort of stuff i i enjoyed i had a guy that taught me british pros and anthology
and he had one time taught mathematics and he said i told dr moore who was president at that
time that i could not teach mathematics anymore because i started reading that poem
and the assyrians swept down he said that just took me but i think they should be the classic
the well-educated man
and i'm not going to be a true alabama our individual reasons for doing the right thing
and i'm not going to be a true alabama i mean at least i'm one of the last ones i met
him when i was in high school and i'm glad they did and he was one of the best students
i've ever met with in my life in addition to the fact that i don't think they should be teaching
mathematics because they should be teaching a lot of other subjects you know the math
and the humanities but also they should be taking their classes in the future and one of the things
that they said that they were going to do
math in the math department and getting them interested in teaching some courses that are
not all just theoretical mathematics but some applied mathematics for the engineers the chemists
and all these other people you should be taking courses and we should have some students taking
some courses in physics and mathematics and and uh and we don't do that because what is going to
happen and even business because what's going to happen is that things are changing so fast
that you don't have time for somebody to come up on the curve anymore you've got to have people
well enough broad enough trained that they we can go from one project to another and he still
doesn't have such a long uh learning curve and i think that this was their feeling that we've been
going in the wrong direction and right now the legislature is still trying to make you take
fewer hours and everything
the best place to learn something is in the classroom it's the most efficient way
and that's what i would say is let's turn out some really well educated well-rounded
that have had some courses in all these other departments uh so that uh and it's not just
in engineering it's all in the arts and sciences and i uh i enjoyed look back over it i enjoyed
some of those courses that i had outside the department far more than i did some of them i
had inside you
the one i had in british pros anthology i wouldn't take anything for that course
and it depends on the teachers you've got to have real good teachers dedicated to teach those
courses and so that's what i would that's what it's going to take because what they've come up
with the concept is that we are now in the age of a learning society and to be one and most nations
are going to adopt this concept because we're going to have to be a learning society and we're
going to have to have a good education so that they can have a pretty good education
for when they go out and they just have to take with them experiences uh and be willing
to take lessons from them because they found out that's the only way that they can keep
the competitive edge on their competitors or stay ahead of them is by learning technology
faster than they do and to learn it faster you've got to have a broad background so you
can switch from one technology to another and pick it up and so i would say that that
and and over a broader scope but now none of these students take courses outside i mentioned
already to you how many i took outside the department then i went over after i got my phd
and took all the courses that they offered in the math department on top of it so
this is the direction we ought to go in so that they can switch fields see i wrote this book on
quantitative cancer model and risk assessment statistics and i never had a course in it but
i had enough mathematics that i could work with silken and try to figure it out but i wrote it so
that engineers could read it and in terms of the department's leadership should it be a department
head or a department chairman oh i think a department head i think you need uh you need
more of a line organization and i'm not sure that the terms ought to be so limited because
uh if you get to putting so much emphasis on just being a professor and doing research the guy said
hell i'll take it
for four years and i'm not going to bust my butt because it's not going to have anything to do with
where i'm going i'll take it and get mine done and then somebody else can have it i think that's
a tendency in chairmanships and i was using the term chairman also to mean that the chairman is
bound by the majority vote yeah i don't think that's a good way to run it uh i usually would
try to every once in a while you're not going to uh well first of all you should be
convincing people that this is direction go in that you don't even really have to vote you can
just look and see what the consensus is and decide what to do and so uh no i i'm uh i think it still
has to be a leadership position and when you get these others you don't really have the leadership
in there that you need people don't really feel responsible for it and i found this out in lab
running the laboratories i had to get somebody permanent
in charge of them because if you didn't they just went downhill one guy would come in he'd be in
charge of it and he wouldn't fix anything because i'm just going to have it this semester somebody
else got it after that the who were the key people in your departmental office
and other staff folks that deserve a pat on the back for their fine work
well actually wanda greer was the one that stayed with me the longest and probably helped me the most
to run through them about one a year until i got a hold of her now that's the wife of john and that
she's from port arthur where you are knows you and she did a real good job because she had good
judgment and also was good at reading anything i wrote and tell me what was wrong and she worked
with me long enough that she didn't mind telling me when she thought i was wrong and did and so
that's worth a lot to get somebody that knows the ropes and will just take take it and run it like a
man when you go to play football you know there's more to it than a man when he's a professional
um so that's worth a lot to get somebody that knows the ropes and we'll just take take it and run it
and just run it on the road if you want to get some of the ropes and we'll just take take it and run it
We'll just take it and run with it.
All right.
